name: Nobottle
description: Nobottle was a small logging village just off the Fairwater road between Little Delving and Bywater. Its Cutters' Guild was known throughout the Westfarthing.

name: Vamag
description: Vamag is a coastal ruin of the mid-Second Age, said to have served as a retreat for Adûnaphel — believed by some to be one of the Nazgûl. The local Coastal Peoples refuse to approach it, declaring the site haunted. The walls stand dark and salt-worn above the cliffs, and the silence around them feels heavier than it should.

name: Undrond
description: Udûdhrond ('Deep-hidden vault') was once a township of 4,300 on the west bank of the Gwathló, about eighty miles downriver from Tharbad. By the mid-Third Age its population had collapsed following the Great Plague and by T.A. 1970 the town was fully abandoned. What remains is a stretch of crumbling stone foundations along the riverbank, half-hidden by vegetation — a haunted silence where a busy market once stood.

name: Ost-in-edhil
description: Ost-in-Edhil was the great Elvish city of Eregion, founded in the Second Age by Galadriel and Celeborn and later the home of Celebrimbor the smith, who forged the Great Rings here. Its ruins still show the elegant stonework of the Gwaith-i-Mírdain craftsmen; broken archways and carved lintels half-buried in ivy suggest a settlement of rare sophistication. Nothing now inhabits those fallen halls but wind and the memory of what was lost when Sauron brought the city to ruin.

name: Bar Algund
description: A small town of western Belfalas, lying close to the great seat of Dol Amroth and within the same Grithion. Bar Algund's stone houses and small harbour face the Bay of Belfalas; the town relies on fishing and on trade with the Prince's court nearby.

name: Caras Amarth
description: Caras Amarth was the first palace of the Woodland Realm, built by King Oropher deep in the forest — a network of caverns carved into a hillside, lit within by lanterns. When Thranduil moved his court northward to the Aradhrynd, Caras Amarth was abandoned; by the mid-Third Age the forest has grown back over the old entrances and only the oldest Elves remember exactly where they lie.

name: Goblin-town
description: Goblin-town is a vast maze of natural caverns near the High Pass, occupied by Orcs since at least the Second Age. The tunnels connect Imladris to the eastern lands and give the Goblins an unmatched ability to ambush travellers crossing the Misty Mountains. Those who have entered and escaped describe a stench of smoke and filth, crowded galleries lit by smoky torches, and the constant noise of Orc voices. The Great Goblin rules here by fear and brute force.

name: Bar Erui
description: A mountain hamlet of no more than thirty-five households in the foothills of the Blue Mountains, known as Eruimar or 'Lonely Home'. Though remote, it is easy enough to spot from a distance. Founded as a Dúnedain trading post for trappers and mountaineers, it retains that character in the mid-Third Age, with a small Laughing Brook running through the settlement. The tavern here can fill a long evening with wild tales from the hills.

name: Barad Othnir
description: A castle and small settlement in northeastern Harithilien, perched on a rocky spur of the western Ephel Dúath. Its tower overlooks the main road below, commanding a view of traffic between Ithilien and Mordor. The castle walls are of dark mountain stone. A small garrison and their families live in the shadow of the keep.

name: Aludor
description: A small town in southwestern Harithilien, set halfway between Hyarpendë and Tir Ethraid on the road through the region. The town is notable as the seat of House Alagdor, one of Ithilien's ancient noble families, whose home castle Târik-Ûriyat rises on a rocky spur above the surrounding fields and olive groves. Stone houses with tiled roofs line the main road; the air carries the scent of herbs and cultivated terraces.

name: Bar Nepos
description: A small town and watchtower at the mouth of the river Nethos in southwestern Harithilien, home of the noble house of Nethos. The tower overlooks the river confluence and the road to Pelargir. The town's houses are compact and stone-built; the watchtower is the tallest structure for miles.

name: Bar Morvegil
description: A small town of Harithilien on a tributary of the Poros, once known as Bar Miruvor ('House of Precious-juice') and renamed Bar Morvegil ('House of the Black-sword') when the local noble family took a martial crest. Its stone houses and small river-wharf serve travellers on the Poros road; orchards and herb-gardens cover the gentle slopes around it.

name: Amon Ereb
description: A lone hill of gentle but commanding slopes in southern Forlindon, the highest point between the Ramdal and the Gelion. In the Third Age, the tower of Car Sidh ('House of Peace') crowns its summit — seat of Rhiannon, a Mithren noblewoman who patronises the largest house of Eldarin healers in Forlindon. About two hundred Elves dwell here; their halls are bright and ordered, and the terraced slopes bear the faint traces of much older First-Age earthworks. The hill is a landmark visible for miles across the flat southern lands.

name: Amon Draug
description: The highest hill in Forlindon, Amon Draug, sat near the mid-point of the Emyn Cûn hills, where the Gelion began. It was possible to see Belegaer from it’s summit.

name: Cebir Luin
description: A steep rocky hill south of the Pinnath Luin in Forlindon, site of a battle in S.A. 1700. The hill's flanks are honeycombed with old Dwarven excavations. By the Third Age it is a ruin: overgrown earthworks, dark dripping tunnels, and travellers passing below glancing up at the jagged stone crest with unease.

name: Barad Caranthit
description: A rocky hilltop in Forlindon south of the Pinnath Luin, honeycombed with old Dwarven excavations. The site of a battle in S.A. 1700 where a band of Dwarves and Elves made a last stand against Orcs. By the Third Age it is a ruin: crumbling walls and overgrown earthworks visited only by the bold or the curious. Old stories say one survivor escaped to rouse a Dwarven host — the tunnels go deeper than they look.

name: Stock
description: Stock sits in the fertile Baranduin valley, its well-tended fields producing some of the best mushrooms in the Shire — a fact the locals are quietly proud of. It is a comfortable Stoor-majority village of low stone-and-turf buildings near the river, settled in the early days of the Shire. Travellers on the road to the Bucklebury ferry pass through here, and the smell of fresh earth and mushroom beds is pleasant in damp weather.

name: Sunthra Unsar
description: Originally built under the name Mistalonde, the city appeared in records from the mid-Second Age until T.A. 1503, when Gordaig of Saerlann captured and renamed it Sunthra Unsar. The rebuilt city became the seat of power of the Saerlannish (Dynion) clan, and its inhabitants were almost entirely hillmen.

name: Bar Nevedui
description: A modest castle of southern Andrast on the river Nevduin, part of the remote county of Bar Morthil. Its walls are of local grey stone, maintained by a small household garrison; the surrounding land is sparsely farmed by Dunir dependants who provide the fort with supplies in exchange for protection from Corsair raiders.

name: Erädas
description: Erädas is a Gondorian castle on a sheltered cove in the Bay of Umbar — built by Eärnil I and later seized by Umbar in T.A. 1449. It now serves as a rural retreat for Lord Teldûmeir and as the seat of local administration. The stone battlements look out over a blue harbour; the Coastal People, Haradan and Corsair inhabitants give it a character quite unlike any Gondorian fortress further north.
inhabitants: 50% Coastal People, 20% Haradan, 20% Corsair, 10% Black Númenórean

name: Isigir
description: Isigir is a castle on the north shore of the Nen Umbar, its surroundings known for the pungent medicinal herbs that grow in the warm lowland soil. Built by the Captains of Umbar in the early Third Age, it serves as the rural power base of Lord Adûmir and administers the productive farmlands around the lake. The herb-gardens are its most distinctive feature; their smell reaches the traveller well before the castle walls are visible.
inhabitants: 60% Coastal People, 25% Corsair, 10% Haradan, 5% Black Númenórean

name: Marös
description: Marös occupies a promontory jutting into the Bay of Umbar — a Númenórean-built castle that has watched the bay's traffic for over two thousand years. The sea breaks directly against its lower walls at high tide. Its current lord is Borathôr of Umbar; the mixed population of Coastal People, Corsairs and Haradan give the place a rough, polyglot character.
inhabitants: 30% Coastal People, 30% Corsair, 30% Haradan, 10% Black Númenórean

name: Michel Delving
description: Michel Delving is the largest town and administrative capital of the Shire, spread across the gentle slopes of the White Downs in the Westfarthing. The Mayor's Hall and the Mathom-house — a repository of Hobbit curiosities — are its two most notable buildings. Most houses are of whitewashed stone with round doors and windows; kitchen gardens and flower beds line every lane. Market day is the social high point of the week, drawing Hobbits from the surrounding villages to sell produce and catch up on news.

name: Cithiel
description: Once a great township of 4,400 on the Baranduin in Minhiriath. By T.A. 1400 the old town was abandoned. The site has since been resettled on a smaller scale by Eriadorian tribesmen, who call it Cuncan — a rough cluster of timber-and-daub buildings amid the foundations of the much larger earlier settlement.

name: Parth Ainatir
description: Parth Aertirith (S."Sward of the Holy Watch") was an Elvish camping glade on the border of Sîrgala and Harlindon.The Place was forsaken by the Elves after a great tragedy, the juvenile and drunk Elcaldil of Girithlin, heir of Girithlin, had harassed an elven girl and was thrown down by Glorfindel Goldenhair, after which the House Girithlin thought of it's family and fate as cursed.

name: Aradhrynd
description: Aradhrynd is the great underground palace of King Thranduil of the Woodland Realm, carved beneath a hill near the northern edge of Mirkwood after the growing Shadow of Dol Guldur made the southern forest uninhabitable. Unlike the dark tunnels of Goblins, the halls are bright with torchlight and alive with Elvish voices; carved pillars support high ceilings, and a river runs through the lower levels. Visitors are rare and rarely welcome — the guards are watchful, and the gates of great iron and oak are seldom opened for strangers.

name: Cerin Amroth
description: Cerin Amroth is a grass-covered hill in the heart of Lórien, crowned by two concentric rings of trees — one of niphredil, one of elanor — whose flowers bloom in gold and white all year. It was designed as a royal retreat by King Amroth and served as the palace of Lothlórien for ages. The traveller allowed inside Lórien finds the hill astonishingly beautiful; the light here is unlike that of the world outside, clear and golden even in winter.

name: Tir Limlight
description: Tir Limlight marks the effective northern limit of Gondorian authority on the Anduin, guarding one of the few crossings at the Undeeps. Founded by Gondorian merchants, it has grown into a walled town of mixed Gondorian and Northman character — stone houses and timber warehouses, the smell of river trade, and the constant awareness of the northern wilderness beyond the walls. The garrison here watches for threats from Rhovanion as much as it watches the river traffic.

name: Fornost Erain
description: Fornost Erain — the 'Fortress of the Kings' — crowns a steep rise on the southern slopes of the North Downs, capital of Arthedain. It is as much fortress as city: concentric rings of stone walls enclose the royal palace, the great library, and the merchant quarters of the Dúnedain. The city's streets are orderly and well-paved; the men who walk them are tall and grave-faced, with the bearing of a people who know themselves to be heirs of Númenór. Beyond the walls, the North Downs roll away in all directions.

name: Dun Fidach
description: What was once a Dúnadan fortress called Amon Mîn ('Hill between the gaps') — a watch-post commanding the Greenway in Dor Wathui — has long since been abandoned. The Rangers of the North maintained it briefly after Arnor's dissolution, but eventually left it to decay. The Dunlendings call the ruins 'Dinas Gwydd'; a small Ranger campsite occasionally shelters here.

name: Dun Udrost
description: Echad Dagoras ('Dagoras' Camp') is an old Elvish ruin south of Eregion, some sixty miles southeast of Ost-in-Edhil. The Rangers of the North use it as a temporary camp; the local Dunlendings call it Dinas Yddryst or Dol Du ('Dark toll') and shun the place. A few broken stone walls and foundation courses amid scrub — eerie in atmosphere.

name: Creatrach
description: In the Coit Cerdd on the borders of Dunfearn stands the ruin of an old Gondorian watchtower called Harndirion ('Southwatch'). The Rangers of the North maintain a semi-permanent camp here among the ancient stones. The local Dunlendings call it Craig-y-Din ('Castle Rock') and give it a wide berth.

name: Daithen
description: A Dunnish clan-hold in Dunfearn near the Misty Mountains, its name meaning 'Ancient Fire'. A rough highland village of timber longhouses and livestock pens, ruled by chieftain Menu with the counsel of the local conjurer Selyf Hen. Smoke from fires hangs low in the valley on cold mornings.

name: Carnach
description: Ost-Dunhoth was once a township of some 3,200 people in Enedhwaith. By T.A. 1400 its population had drained away entirely. What remains is a field of overgrown ruins — broken walls of old stone half-buried in scrub and grass, with no permanent inhabitants. Local Dunlendings call the place cursed and do not build here.

name: Calost
description: A village of Anórien straddling the Calenen stream on the Great North Road, about thirty miles from Calenhad and twelve from Min-Rimmon. The ancient stone bridge over the Calenen is still in excellent repair. Stone houses on the western bank cluster around the Traveller's Rest Inn, a well-regarded stopping point for anyone on the road. A mill draws from the river; market days bring peasants from surrounding villages.

name: Astirian
description: The ancestral manor of House Astorion, a noble Gondorian family of Anórien, on the fringes of the Mouths of the Entwash north of Min-Rimmon. The manor is a solid Gondorian country house of dressed stone, surrounded by extensive farmlands and pastures. The Astorion family has held these lands since the early Third Age; their hall stands as a quiet landmark on the flat green plain.

name: Bar Duilin
description: A town of Anórien situated on the Anduin opposite the island fortress of Cair Andros. Its location makes it a natural waystation for soldiers, messengers and supplies crossing to and from the fortress; a garrison camp and a river-landing dominate the waterfront. Stone houses and wooden barracks line the main road leading to the ferry.

name: Bar-i-Dinuviel
description: Bar-i-Dinuviel (S. Home of the Nightingale; Os. Mbar Tinduvela) was a fishing village on the coast of Dor Tinuviel.

name: Lond Rhaen
description: A small fishing village on the northwestern coast of Andrast, sheltered in a safe bay of Rast Rhaen. A handful of stone-walled houses face the water; the people here are Dunir fisher-folk whose boats rarely venture far from the bay's protection.

name: Bareketta
description: Bareketta is a handsome estate on the eastern shore of Nenuial, the primary seat of the Eketta family — one of the two most powerful noble clans in Arthedain. It was built with beauty as much as defence in mind: graceful stone towers look out over the lake, and the gardens between the walls are carefully tended. The estate is comfortable and well-staffed; those granted admittance find a household that takes hospitality seriously.

name: Celebannon
description: Celebannon is a small but elegant Elvish village on the banks of the Taurduin in Mirkwood, founded in the early Third Age as a point of contact between the Elves of the Woodland Realm and the surrounding folk of Rhovanion. Its buildings are finely made — carved wood and white stone among the trees — and the Elves here are more accustomed to the presence of travellers than most of their kin. A small market operates occasionally where Elvish goods can be obtained at fair prices.

name: Bar Gilraen
description: A small town of Lebennin on the lower Gilrain, several leagues northeast of Linhir. It is the seat of the noble family of Bar Gilraen, who hold the surrounding county. Neat stone houses with flat or tiled roofs face a central square; the Gilrain's fertile banks support grain-growing and some wool trade.

name: Diniath
description: A village in the County of Gilrain in southwestern Lebennin, the main settlement of the small barony of the same name. Stone houses and a small market square line the road; the surrounding farmland and fishing on the Gilrain tributary support a modest population.

name: Paths Of The Dead
description: The Paths of the Dead are natural caverns beneath the White Mountains, connecting the vale of Dunharrow to the Morthond valley — once used as royal burial chambers by the Daen Coentis, last interred in T.A. 679. The entrance is known, but no living person willingly passes inside. Locals speak of cold air, a sense of invisible presences, and sounds that have no natural explanation. The broken Oath of the Dead men lingers here; the caverns are considered one of the most accursed places in Gondor.

name: Dol Calimir
description: A town of Lebennin on a small tributary of the Serni, halfway between Minas Brethil and Pelargir. Its position on this route makes it a regular stopping point for travellers. Stone houses and a small inn; in recent years the town has been fortified with a new outer wall.

name: Barfalas
description: A small fishing village in Lebennin on the Anduin, southwest of Pelargir. Stone houses with fishing nets hung to dry face a sheltered cove; the people here fish the river and trade their catch at the nearest market. The village is small and tranquil.

name: Arnbad
description: A small town in eastern Methed Lebennin, seat of the surrounding Grithion of the same name. Its name — 'Royal Path' in Sindarin — hints at an old road that once ran through its centre. Stone houses and a modest market hall ring a crossroads shaded by plane trees; the surrounding farmland yields grain and olives.

name: Mulkan
description: Mulkan is a Lossoth settlement in the frozen wastes of Forochel, established as a seasonal base for hunting and gathering. In summer, it consists of hide tents and driftwood shelters around a communal fire; in winter, the Lossoth build their remarkable snow-and-ice houses, low-roofed and surprisingly warm inside. Strangers are mistrusted, but those who arrive with trade goods — iron, cloth, salt — may be received with cautious hospitality.

name: Bar Luindol
description: A small town of Lossarnach, situated along a tributary of the Erui a few leagues south of Minas Arthoron on the Rathon Gondor. It is the ancestral seat of the noble family of Bar Luindol. Its stone houses and cultivated terraces benefit from the rich soil of Lossarnach.

name: Echorost
description: A fortified village of southwestern Lossarnach at the confluence of the Celos and the Sirith, the chief settlement of the county of Enedhîr. Its name — 'Ring-fort' — reflects its circular defensive layout: stone walls and a central square that serves as market and muster ground. The surrounding farmland of Lossarnach is rich; the village exports grain, wool and the famous roses of the valley.

name: Nansret
description: Nansret is the only known village of the Beffraen, the Wild Men of the Rast Vorn. It is a rough settlement of bark shelters and timber lean-tos in the densest part of the forest, deliberately difficult to find. The Beffraen do not welcome outsiders; the village exists to support their hunting and gathering, and its location shifts with the seasons.

name: Romenost
description: Romenost is a Gondorian waystation on the Men Romen, built by the Eastern Army to defend the road against incursions from the east. Its stone walls and barracks are practical rather than impressive; the garrison is small and the surrounding steppe is empty and windswept. Travellers on the Men Romen are glad to see it — it is the only shelter for many miles.

name: Cirith Ungol
description: Cirith Ungol is a Gondorian fortress high in the Ephel Dúath, built to watch the Morgai and the Plateau of Gorgoroth from a commanding vantage point. Its towers stand on a narrow pass above the road into Mordor; the view from its battlements encompasses a vast dark landscape of ash and broken rock. The garrison is small and dwindling — King Tarondor has considered abandoning the post — but for now, the Gondorian flag still flies above the gate.

name: Burh Beorills
description: The seat of the Berilas clan along the Men Rómen in the hills of Duil Rewinion, known in Rohirric as Berilabaurg or 'Little-bears' Town'. A stockaded Northman settlement of timber halls and longhouses; its cattle-pens and storehouses sit behind a wooden palisade. The Berilas are tough hill-folk; their hall is hung with bear-skins and old weapons.

name: Cirith Gorgor
description: Cirith Gorgor (S. "Haunted Pass") was a high pass where the mountain ranges of the Ered Lithui and Ephel Dúath met in the far north-western corner of Mordor. The pass opened out into the mountain vale of Udûn, and was guarded from the beginning of the Third Age by the Towers of the Teeth. When Sauron returned to Mordor c. TA 2951 the Morannon was rebuilt across the Pass.

name: Athrad Poros
description: The ford settlement of Athrad Poros was an important stop on the land trade route south. It began as a military garrison protecting the border between the nascent Kingdom-in-Exile and the Umbarean sphere of influence. When the Harnen Corridor opened, Athrad Poros became a critical juncture for trade with Near Harad and Khand.

name: Car Gaul
description: A freehold estate in the County of Nan Lefnui, on the lower Lefnui. It is the property of the Bannor family, who farm the surrounding fields and pastures of this remote corner of Gondor's western frontier. The manor house is a solid stone farmstead with a small courtyard and stableyard.

name: Cair Andros
description: Cair Andros (S. Ship of Long Foam) was an island fortress on the River Anduin, roughly forty miles north of Osgiliath. Shaped like a great ship with a high rocky prow pointing north, it was fortified by Steward Túrin II to protect Anórien from enemy crossings. It served as a vital northern bulwark of Gondor against Mordor throughout the late Third Age.

name: Eredimar
description: Eredimar (S. Mountain Home) lay at the foot of the Ered Luin within the valley of Imladorn (S. The Wooded Valley). Despite housing fewer than 100 people, it was remarkable for its cultural diversity — Hobbits, Dwarves, Elves, and Men had all settled here at various times. Sulfur springs nearby gave Eredimar renown as a place of healing, and three well-maintained lodges welcomed travelers.

name: Filimer
description: Filimer lies in silent ruin, its crumbled walls barely distinguishable from the surrounding landscape. Locals say it was once a settlement of some size, but no record survives of who built it or when it fell. Travellers passing through see only broken stone half-swallowed by grass and thorny scrub.

name: Bar Irlossiel
description: Ruined castle. Barad Perinluoss, commonly known as the Silent Castle, was an ancient Elven refuge in southern Sîrgala — an independent community of Wood-Elves and Arthadan religious refugees who pledged fealty to a half-Elf known as Iralussiel the Tranquil. Iralussiel died mysteriously around the 11th or 12th century T.A., and the shelter became eerily peaceful thereafter.

name: Nan i Naugrim
description: The greatest mine of Nan-i-Naugrim and the capital of the Broadbeam Dwarves since the Second Age. By the mid-Third Age, Edennogrod — the largely ruined Khazad above Nan-i-Naugrim — stood as a shadow of its former self. At the end of the Third Age it was ruled by the feudal lord Grár, who held the honorary title Mountain King among the Shire-folk.

name: Amrûnaur
description: Amrûnaur is a Gondorian castle on a rocky shelf projecting from the mountain called Thoredarb by the Eredrim, commanding the central pass of Dor-en-Ernil. The fortress is notable for its lightning rods — iron spikes mounted on the towers to deflect the violent storms that frequently pass through the pass. The Dunnish population of the surrounding hills views the garrison with wariness; the castle in turn keeps a close eye on movement through the pass.

name: Bar Lithyrn
description: Bar Lithyrn is a mining village on the slopes of the northern Ered Lithui, founded by Gondorian commercial interests to extract silver ore from the mountains. Most inhabitants are local Donaen men lured by the prospect of wages; their Dúnadan lords ship the silver south to Gondor's treasuries. The village is isolated and in slow decline — the silver veins are thinning, and each year a few more families leave for easier lives elsewhere.

name: Bar Adorn
description: Bar Adorn was a settlement on the River Adorn near the western borders of Rohan, serving as a minor trading post between the Rohirrim and the Dunlendings of the region.

name: Saerthondost
description: Saerthondost was one of two Dúnedain strongholds protecting Pinnath Gelin and Nan Lefnui from the wilds of Hairaverkien. Located on the north bank of the Lefnui where the river turned sharply northwest, it was named for the nearby swamps rich in shepherd's herbs. A lighthouse tower linked it to its sister fortress Pinnornost to the northeast; it was founded in T.A. 823 and abandoned in T.A. 2157.

name: Adavalinda
description: A small village on the eastern bank of the Lefnui, where the river meets the Rámanen confluence. It serves as a staging point for travellers seeking to cross the White Mountains via the Cirith Iaur, some forty miles to the northwest. A ferry here is the last easy crossing before the river narrows to flat-bottomed-boat country upstream. Stone and timber houses cluster around the landing, their occupants largely dependent on guiding travellers and working the river.

name: Dol Guldur
description: Dol Guldur — 'Hill of Sorcery' — rises from the southern extremity of Mirkwood on the summit of Amon Lanc, an extinct cinder cone. Since around T.A. 1050 it has been occupied by a power called the Necromancer, who has filled the fortress with Orcs and Trolls and spread a shadow over the southern forest. The hill is wrapped in a permanent gloom; the trees around it are dead or twisted, and Mirkwood's darkness is deepest in its shadow. No traveller ventures near willingly.

name: Creb Durga
description: Creb Durga was carved by the Warlord's Trolls from a craggy hill about seventy miles north of Sudúri — a hidden base for raids across central Cardolan. Its location is deliberately obscure; travellers in the region have disappeared without explanation, taken by the Warlord's parties operating from this base.

name: Aglarond
description: Aglarond is a system of extensive caverns in the northern slopes of the White Mountains, currently used as a refuge and storehouse for the garrison at nearby Ostiras. First sheltered by Númenórean settlers long ago, the caves have never been fully explored — deeper galleries glitter with untouched gem deposits that serve as an emergency financial reserve for Gondor's kings. The entrance is carefully guarded and not advertised.

name: Dol Amroth
description: Dol Amroth — named for King Amroth of Lórien who drowned offshore in T.A. 1981 — is the great coastal city and seat of the Prince of Dor-en-Ernil. Its white towers rise from a high cliff above the bay; the harbour below is one of Gondor's finest, home to the warships and tall-masted trading vessels of the southern fleet. The city has an Elvish quality to its older quarters — graceful stonework and the lingering influence of the Elvish settlement that once stood here — while the newer docks ring with the noise of any busy port.

name: Moria
description: Moria — Khazad-dûm to the Dwarves — is the greatest Dwarven city ever built, delved beneath three peaks of the central Misty Mountains. In its heyday it housed tens of thousands; even now, with its population much reduced, the inhabited halls are vast and busy with the sound of hammers and the smell of forges. Travellers who have passed through its western gate describe colonnaded halls of astonishing size, veins of mithril visible in the rock face, and a beauty that is severe and magnificent at once.

name: Frogmorton
description: Frogmorton is a comfortable Hobbit village on the Great East Road in the Eastfarthing, its central position on the road making it a natural stopping point for travellers crossing the Shire. The local farmers bring their produce to market here on regular days; a decent inn serves the road traffic. The surrounding land is well-farmed and peaceful, typical of the Eastfarthing's prosperous character.

name: Beldwin
description: Beldwin is a Dúnadan castle and its surrounding settlement in the northern Westmarch, just south of the River Isen near an ancient stone bridge. It serves as the administrative base for this corner of the region and maintains a garrison that keeps order among the mixed Dunman and Dúnadan population. The castle is solid and well-maintained; the bridge beside it carries considerable traffic between the Westmarch and the lands to the north.

name: Cillien
description: Cillien is a Dunnish village in the hills of Enedwaith, the focal point for the scattered families of Clan Eogbann. Its most notable building is the Healer's Hall — a timber longhouse where the clan's healers practise their craft, serving anyone in the region who makes the journey here. The village itself is small and self-contained, its longhouses arranged in a rough circle around a shared fire-pit.

name: Kuska
description: Kuska sits in the Angmar Gap three miles west of Barad Morkai, founded by Angmar's forces to keep the fortress supplied. The village is a grim place: timber barracks and storehouses surrounded by a rough palisade, populated almost entirely by the mixed Mannish people — Dunnish, Brygath Easterlings, Estaravë — who serve the Witch-king's armies. Provisions are moved constantly up the road to Morkai; Kuska's only purpose is to feed the war machine above it.
inhabitants: 100% Angmarean (Mixed Mannish: Dunnish, Brygath Easterling, Estaravë)

name: Minas Girithlin
description: Minas Girithlin is a stout castle set in the hills, ancestral home of the Princes of Dol Girithlin, built around T.A. 590 to dominate the surrounding countryside. Its walls have withstood several sieges without being breached; the garrison is loyal and experienced. The Prince's hall within is comfortable if not luxurious — a working noble's house, with stables and a well-stocked armoury.

name: Culwic
description: Culwic, originally known as Himtil or Himobel (S. Chill Village) and later as Colwich, was a small town in western Arthedain on the Little Lune, just north of the borders of Dor Lelvin and Lindon. Its mixed population of Men and Dwarves supported the dwarven settlements to the north through industry and agriculture.

name: Vidusbaurg
description: Buhr Widu, situated near the small Forest Lake, retained some of the royal splendor of the days of Vidugavia. The Grammuz princes who ruled it after the Plague lacked the strength to restore its former glory. Like most Northman villages on the plains, it consisted of long wooden and thatched houses defended by an earthen wall and sturdy wooden towers.

name: Morkai
description: Barad Morkai rises from a granite bluff above the Angmar Road — a black-stone fortress built by the Witch-king to monitor all movement entering and leaving the Nan Angmar. The road passes directly beneath its walls; travellers feel the weight of the fortress's watch as they pass. A large garrison occupies it permanently; the broad plains to the south serve as Orc training grounds.

name: Minas Thonfalon
description: Minas Thonfalon is a ruined Arnorian watchtower on the 'Bog Coast' of the Rast Vorn, built in T.A. 503 to watch traffic along the coast. Its foundations are slowly sinking into the waterlogged ground; the upper courses have already tumbled into the bog. On foggy days the broken tower is barely visible above the reed-beds, and local fishermen give it a wide berth.

name: Bar Morthil
description: A royal estate at the westernmost tip of Andrast — the farthest habitation in all of Gondor. A single stone manor overlooks the sheer sea-cliffs of Ras Morthil, where the ocean crashes hundreds of feet below. The estate is maintained year-round by a warden and servants; it faces west toward the open sea, built by the sons of Elendil as a memorial to drowned Númenór. Kings and Stewards sometimes come here for solitude.

name: Amrûnaur
description: A second fortification in the Dor-en-Ernil pass bearing the same name as Amrûnaur to the south — a commanding position on a rocky shelf, with lightning rods on its towers and a watchful garrison that monitors movement through the mountain route.

name: Azanulinbar-dûm
description: Azanulinbar-dûm is the principal mansion of the Dwarves of the Iron Hills and the seat of Lord Fulla III. First delved around T.A. 1000, it has grown into a full Dwarven settlement of homes, workshops and mines beneath the Emyn Angren. Outsiders are rarely admitted; those few who have seen the interior describe halls of bare stone brightened by crystal formations, and the constant sound of industry echoing from every gallery.

name: Guinnion
description: The village of Guinnion has long since been abandoned — a scattering of mossy foundation stones and choked lanes where a small community once lived. The site lies quietly off the road, overlooked by all but the most curious travellers.

name: Bodroth
description: Bodroth was a city of Eketyar in Arthedain, situated on the northern shores of Lake Evendim about forty miles north of Annúminas. It had a population of roughly 4,900 in T.A. 250, but declined through the Third Age and was eventually abandoned, though it survived the fall of the last King by several generations.

name: Cirith Dudhrandir
description: Cirith Dudhrandir was the only major mountain pass through the Belfalas Peninsula and the only one with a paved road, making it strategically vital for Gondor's southern defenses. At 5,800 feet and over sixty miles east to west, it was guarded by the fortress of Naur Amrûn. The pass was named in S.A. 3400 when Edhelion of Eldalonde received Belfalas as a fief.

name: Cathiel
description: Ruined city. Cathiel was a settlement of Ethir Gwathló in Cardolan, about seventy-five miles inland from the mouths of the Gwathló. It had a population of roughly 4,000 in T.A. 250, declining through the Third Age and finally abandoned by T.A. 1640.

name: Buhr Ailgra
description: Buhr Ailgra is one of the few permanent settlements of the Éothraim, a fortified town on the lower plains of Rhovanion enclosed by a stout earthen rampart. Most of its residents use it as a seasonal base, following their herds through the rest of the year; the town is most crowded in winter and nearly empty in midsummer. Thatch-and-turf houses cluster inside the rampart; the permanent residents are traders and craftsmen who supply the Éothraim when they return.

name: Barak-shathur
description: Founded by settlers from Azanulinbar-dûm c. T.A. 1490.. Exploitation of the mineral resources of the Iron Hills.. The Khuzdul name of the stronghold is thought to mean 'Axe Mountain' in the Westron.

name: Lond Daer
description: Established as a colony of Númenor by Aldarion in the ninth century of the Second Age.. A Númenórean haven on the shores of Endor.. Lond Daer was ruined by a cataclysmic tidal wave in S.A. 3319.

name: Tilmendir
description: Built by Gondor, circa T.A. 991.. Surveillance of traffic passing from Westmarch to Anfalas; a show of authority for the local Dunnish tribes.. Tilmendir is located ten miles south of the town Derwath.

name: Dundok
description: Dundok is a Stoor Hobbit village hidden in the drier edges of the Gladden Fields, occupied since at least T.A. 1360. Its low-roofed smials and reed-thatched houses are almost invisible among the reeds and sedge grass — which suits the inhabitants perfectly, as they have little desire for outside visitors. The village sustains itself through small-scale agriculture and the fishing of the Gladden's quieter channels.

name: Iant Methed
description: Iant Methed (The Last Bridge) was built of stone with three great arches at the height of Arnorian power in T.A. 320. In the petty wars of Rhudaur the crossing was generally considered neutral ground. The Last Inn, long run by the Grumm family, stood nearby; just northwest lay the ruins of Dol Elerilde, stronghold of an old lord of Rhudaur.

name: Laur Dunnen
description: Larach Duhnnan lay on territory of Clan Feargan but the flow of foreign traffic made it a neutral ground for inter-tribal discussions and trade. It had no walls, relying on the nearby Feargan fortress for defense, and sat at one of the few accessible crossings of the Dunstrem, for which the clan lords charged a modest toll.

name: Greenholm
description: A quiet outpost in the middle Third Age, home to a few Arthadan families coexisting with nearby Elves who observed the comings and goings of mortals on behalf of the lords of the Grey Havens. One of the Elves, Sinda Calenlinn, was an expert herbalist fond of fermented grains.

name: Enerond
description: Ruined city. Enerond was a township of Brethyar in Arthedain, on the east bank of the Lune River. It had a population of about 5,100 in T.A. 250, declining through the Third Age until abandoned before its end.

name: Eithel Thurin
description: Eithel Thurin is a Gondorian garrison-town in Harondor, founded by the army in T.A. 932 to extend Gondor's control over this contested coastal region. It serves as a trading centre for coastal traffic and the main base for Gondorian authority between the Poros and Harnen rivers. The town is frequently raided by Umbar and hostile Haradrim; its walls have been repaired many times, and the population has a watchful, embattled quality.

name: Towers Of The Teeth
description: The Towers of the Teeth — Narchost and Carchost — flank the Cirith Gorgor on either side of the Morannon, built by Gondor in the first year of the Third Age to observe all traffic attempting to enter or leave Mordor. They are forbidding structures of black stone on bare crags; their garrison peers down at the ash-plain of Udûn below and reports any movement. No one passes into Mordor without being seen.

name: Andrath
description: Andrath was founded in T.A. 860 by Thorondur of Cardolan and served as the administrative seat of the Prince of Dol Tinare; it has been ruined and rebuilt many times. Most recently it was sacked by King Broggha's Hillmen. The ruins that remain are a palimpsest of different building periods — Arnorian stonework, Cardolani additions, and hasty Hillman repairs — now largely abandoned.

name: Bar-en-tinnen
description: Bar-en-tinnen is a small village on the east bank of the Anduin, grown up around a mill on the Sir Tinnen stream. Founded around T.A. 1512, it provides inland communities with fish and serves as a modest trading point for local farmers. The mill wheel turns constantly; the smell of grain and river-mud is constant.

name: Onodrith
description: Onodrith grew around a ferry crossing of the Entwash in the tenth century of the Third Age, receiving a royal charter in T.A. 1240. It is a small market town and local administrative centre, handling most of the river traffic moving between Gondor's northern and western provinces. Stone warehouses and timber wharves line the riverside; the ferryman's charges are posted on a board at the landing.

name: Minas Arthor
description: Minas Arthor, once the capital of Arnach, was situated at the crossings of the Erui, where a settlement had existed since the earliest chronicles. Founded at the beginning of the Third Age, it grew into the center of a prosperous agricultural district.

name: Bar Astirian
description: Astorion Manor (S. Bâr Astorion) was the former home of House Astorion, a noble Gondorian family. It was situated on the outskirts of the Entwash Mouths, north of Min-Rimmon, where the family held extensive lands.

name: Nothva Rhaglaw
description: Nothva Rhaglaw is the only permanent Hillman town west of the Mitheithel — founded by Broggha of Rhudaur in T.A. 1639 as a trading centre and point of local justice. It is a rough, busy place by the standards of the region: a market where Hillmen, Dúnedain and various others conduct business under the rough authority of Broggha's appointed men. The buildings are timber-and-daub longhouses; the market square is unpaved and muddy in rain.

name: Gram
description: The Witch-King's servants erected this mountain fortress on the southern branch of the Misty Mountains forming the boundary of Nan Angmar. Gram was the most formidable Orc stronghold west of the mountains; no man had ever escaped its depths to give a coherent account of its interior.

name: Bar Engui
description: A small hamlet on the river Engui in Andrast, one of the most remote corners of Gondor. A handful of stone-walled garths cluster near the river; the people here are Dunir fishermen and small farmers who seldom see strangers.

name: Fangoth
description: Fangoth is a ruin now, its walls toppled and its streets empty. Once a city of some standing, it was abandoned after the troubles that plagued this region in the middle Third Age. Only broken archways and the outlines of old streets remain visible beneath the weeds.

name: Minas Tirith
description: Originally called Minas Anor (S. Tower of the Sun), Minas Tirith was built at the foot of Mount Mindolluin in the Ered Nimrais. The city rose in seven concentric tiers of white stone, each gate offset from the last, and the White Tower housed the last Palantír in Gondor's keeping. Together with Osgiliath and Minas Ithil, it formed the core of Gondor.

name: Ossarnen
description: Ossarnen is a Gondorian mining town in the White Mountains, established in the Second Age and grown since. Its population works the mineral seams of the surrounding mountains; the settlement sits well off the main road, connected to it by a rough track. Stone houses and mine-head structures crowd the narrow valley floor; the sound of picks and hammers carries far in the clear mountain air.

name: Dunlostir
description: Dunlostir is the centre of the cereal trade for central Calenardhon, with a market recorded since T.A. 808. Grain from the surrounding plains is gathered and stored here before being shipped east or south. The town's granaries are its most prominent buildings — large stone structures that dwarf the modest houses around them. On market days the roads leading in are busy with carts and droves.

name: Barad-dûr
description: Barad-dûr was Sauron's great stronghold on a spur of the Ered Lithui, fashioned by the power of the One Ring. Its ruin after the Last Alliance was total — the ring-energies that sustained it departed with Sauron's defeat — yet even the broken remains are vast and dark, far larger than any other structure in Mordor. No one inhabits the ruins; they are avoided even by Orcs, who feel the absence of their master's presence as a wound.

name: Mithlond
description: Mithlond — the Grey Havens — comprises two harbours at the end of the Gulf of Lhûn: Mithannúnlond on the west shore and Mithrónalond on the east. Founded by Círdan after the drowning of Beleriand, it is the principal Elvish settlement in the west of Middle-earth, a place of shipbuilding and of departure for those sailing to the Undying Lands. Its stone quays are graceful and always busy with some quiet Elvish industry; tall ships lie at anchor in the fjord, their white sails furled.

name: Barad-wath
description: Barad Wath is an unfinished Gondorian tower built to monitor traffic between Nurn and Gorgoroth, begun after the fall of Sauron and abandoned when resources ran short. Its dark stone walls rise only partway to their intended height; the scaffolding marks are still visible. The structure stands as a monument to Gondor's ambitions in Mordor — imposing in outline but hollow inside, increasingly neglected.

name: Argonath
description: The Argonath are two colossal figures of Isildur and Anárion carved into the cliffs flanking the chasm at the northern entrance to Nen Hithoel, completed in T.A. 1340 by Romendacil II to mark Gondor's northern border. The figures stand with raised hands in a gesture of forbiddance toward the north; their faces are stern and ancient-seeming even for statues only centuries old. Travellers approaching by river fall silent when they come into view — the scale is humbling.

name: Gundabad
description: Mount Gundabad was a sacred site of the Dwarves and an important enclave in the northern Misty Mountains. It was seized by Orcs multiple times and eventually became the capital of the northern Orc-realm, a constant threat to the free peoples of the north.

name: Nadior
description: Ruined city. Nadior was a settlement of Ethir Gwathló in Cardolan, in a small bay on the Minhiriath coast about seventy miles west of the Gwathló mouths. It had a population of roughly 4,900 in T.A. 250, declining through the mid-Third Age until finally abandoned by T.A. 1640.

name: Tirith Nindir
description: An ancient Gondorian fortress built to protect and guard the approaches to the Dagorlad plain.

name: Lond Angren
description: Lond Angren (S. Iron Port) began as a small Númenórean outpost of the Second Age and grew into a city and lordship controlling western Enedwaith near the mouth of the River Angren.

name: Bar-en-tinnen
description: A second Bar-en-tinnen settlement entry (see id=1183) — a village on the east bank of the Anduin around a mill on the Sir Tinnen, providing fish and farm-produce trade for inland communities.

name: Fanuilond
description: Fanuilond is a Gondorian fishing village on the Ethir Anduin, occupied continuously since the early Third Age and repeatedly targeted by Corsair raids. Its prime location at the mouth of the Anduin keeps drawing people back despite the danger; the fishermen here are experienced and tough, and the harbour — though small — handles a surprising volume of river trade. The town's walls have been rebuilt more than once.

name: Archet
description: Archet lies on the edge of the Chetwood in Bree-land, founded by settlers from the main Bree community in T.A. 1420. It is a purely agricultural village, devoted to growing grain and vegetables for the local market. Low stone-and-timber farmhouses face the road; the Chetwood stands dark and close to the east. Archet folk are generally more suspicious of strangers than Bree itself, and the village has a quiet, self-contained character.

name: Osgiliath
description: Osgiliath was the founding capital of Gondor, built by Elendil's sons on both banks of the Anduin at its widest point. Its great dome once housed a palantír; the Bridge of Osgiliath spanned the river in a single graceful arch. The city has suffered enormously — a great part was burned during the Kin-strife and the palantír was lost in the river — and by the mid-Third Age much of it stands abandoned or in ruins. The inhabited quarters on the western bank retain a ghost of former grandeur.

name: Bar Lithyrn
description: A duplicate entry for Bar Lithyrn — a Gondorian silver-mining village on the northern Ered Lithui, in slow decline as the ore veins thin (see id=1184).

name: Belegost
description: Belegost was one of the two great Dwarf-cities of the Ered Luin, founded before the Sun and Moon first rose. It was destroyed at the end of the First Age when the mountains were broken in the War of Wrath. Its ruins lie buried deep beneath fallen rock and centuries of debris; a few bold Dwarven explorers have recently begun probing the outer passages in hopes of recovering ancestral artefacts, but the deeper halls remain inaccessible.

name: Tumnogoth Iaur
description: The Dwarves of Tumnogath were poor and secretive. Many of their young had emigrated to the distant Iron Hills, and the community stagnated over a few generations. The remaining Dwarven population consisted largely of treasure-seekers and travelling tinkers.

name: Esgaroth
description: Esgaroth — the Lake-town — is built on great timber platforms above the Long Lake, connected to land by a single bridge. Founded as a seasonal trading station in the early Third Age, it has grown into the chief market of northern Rhovanion, where goods from Dale, Erebor, the Woodland Realm and the wide plains are exchanged. The platforms creak pleasantly underfoot; smoke from cook-fires drifts across the water; the Master's Hall rises in the centre of the floating town, its banner visible from the lakeshore.

name: Edoras
description: Edoras (R. The Courts) was the capital of Rohan, built upon an ancient hill site at the northern edge of the White Mountains. The great hall Meduseld crowned the hill, and the kings of the Mark were buried in twin rows of barrows outside the main gate. The surrounding treeless plains gave its lookouts a commanding view of all approaches.

name: Firaen
description: Firaen is a ghost of a settlement — crumbling walls, collapsed rooftops, and silence. Whatever community lived here was driven away or died out long ago, and nature has been slowly reclaiming the stone ever since.

name: Dale
description: Dale is a handsome town of Urban Northmen set in a sheltered valley beneath the Lonely Mountain, seat of the Thyn and a manufacturing centre for the rural Northmen of the region. Its stone houses and workshops are famous for their fine toys and clockwork mechanisms; market-day brings traders from as far as Esgaroth and beyond. The mountain behind the town gives it a dramatic backdrop and a sense of permanence.

name: Litash
description: Litash guards the eastern approaches to Angmar from its position at the mountain's foot, garrisoned by a large force of the Witch-king's mixed Mannish troops. A training centre for priests of the Dark Religion and a base for tax-collection in eastern Angmar, it is a grim town of barracks and ritual halls. The priests who walk its streets wear dark robes; the ordinary soldiers are rough and watchful. Travellers approaching from the east are stopped and questioned before being allowed to pass.

name: Bottoms Bridge
description: Bottoms Bridge is a small Northman village on a low island at the edge of the Gladden Marshes, founded in the mid-Third Age to supply traders using the Anduin. Its timber bridges connect it to the bank; the sound of the river is constant and the smell of marsh-grass and damp wood is ever-present. The few dozen inhabitants live by fishing and by managing the river traffic that passes through.

name: Forlond
description: Forlond is the southern Elvish haven of Lindon, hidden at the head of a steep fjord on the eastern shore of the Gulf of Lhûn. Founded after the ruin of Beleriand, it is primarily a shipbuilding settlement — the sound of hammers on timber and caulking mallets is heard on every visit. The Elves here are of mixed kindred and more accustomed to mortal visitors than most Elvish settlements, though the haven is not on any road and is difficult to find.

name: Aglarond
description: A second entry for Aglarond — the cavern refuge and storehouse in the White Mountains used by the garrison at Ostiras. Its gem-deposits and storage halls are maintained in readiness for emergencies.

name: Faemar
description: Faemar was a village set midway along the long, narrow Taur Galen, at a ford crossing the river. The steep valley downstream made farming impossible; villagers subsisted on wild game, berries, herbs, nuts, and wood for charcoal.

name: Ambon Noirinyaron
description: Ambon Noirinyaron — once a grand Elvish mansion — is now little more than overgrown foundations and a few standing columns. The dressed stonework suggests a high Elvish craft; vines and moss cover what remains. The place has an air of melancholy stillness.

name: Woodmen Village
description: A small village of the Woodmen in the Upper Anduin Vales, partially built on large platforms in the trees in the Elven fashion, reflecting the friendly relations between the Woodmen and the nearby Wood-Elves.

name: Arailt
description: Arailt Hold was the ancient seat of the Dunlending Arailt clan. By the time of the War of the Ring it had long been abandoned; the Rohirrim had established a scouting camp above the ruins and nearby caves.

name: Fennas Drunnin
description: A Council directed most affairs in Fennas Drúnin, a town that depended on local farms and vineyards for its prosperity. Some Hobbits lived nearby but generally avoided contact with the townspeople. Far from the main roads of Cardolan, it was a good base for anyone wishing to explore southern Rhudaur or the Elven ruins of Eregion.

name: Faranel
description: Faranel is a ruined city in the northern lands, its once-inhabited quarters now exposed to wind and rain. The scale of the ruins suggests a sizeable population at its height; by the mid-Third Age there is not a single inhabited building remaining.

name: Rildolach
description: Rildolach lies in ruin among the hills, its old streets grown over. Travellers who venture close enough to see it clearly describe a settlement of Arnorian-era stonework, collapsed and overgrown, with no inhabitants and no sign of recent occupation.

name: Aerost
description: A coastal fortress on the Rathon Gondor in Anfalas, some forty miles west of Lond Galen. Built after the Great Raid on Pelargir (T.A. 1634) to defend the coast between the Celgalen and the Lefnui, Aerost is a key link in Anfalas's chain of coastal beacon towers. From its walls, watchmen can warn neighbouring settlements of Corsair raids within hours. The fort's stone battlements are weathered by salt wind, and a small settlement of soldiers and their families huddles in its shadow.

name: Herubar Gûlar
description: Herubar Gûlar is a keep of uncertain origin — possibly Númenórean — on the Mitheithel, controlling traffic along and across the river. Its age means its stonework is of high quality, but it has clearly changed hands many times; the current arrangements inside are rough and military. The keep is reputed to be haunted by the spirits of previous garrisons, and the soldiers stationed here are uneasy in the lower levels.

name: Anghul
description: Anghul ('Iron-wind' in Sindarin) is one of the six great fortresses of Angmar, situated on a hill above the Anghir below the escarpment of Nan Angmar. It broods over the Anghir Vale and controls the No Man's Land. A garrison of 300 men watches for spies; the commander is one of the Witch-king's Ashâktur. Dark stone walls; the wind off the escarpment is bitter and constant. The Black Raven on Red is the garrison's banner.

name: Shedûn
description: Shedûn is a Witch-king fortress overlooking the broad plains below Angmar, used for regional administration and as a mustering point for the Orc forces trained on the plain below. Its stone walls are of Angmarim construction — heavy and dark — and the view from the battlements encompasses a vast training ground where Orc companies drill with a grim efficiency.

name: Angrenost
description: Angrenost — known to later ages as Isengard — is a Gondorian fortified complex in the Gap of Calenardhon, its defences centred on the indestructible tower of Orthanc. A vast circular wall one mile in diameter encloses the Orthanc and its surrounding grounds; no siege weapon has ever damaged either the tower or the ring-wall. The fortress administers the northwestern approaches to Gondor and maintains a watch on all movement through the Gap.

name: Dol Gwaer
description: A lordly manor of the Nan Celgalen in western Anfalas, seat of a minor Gondorian noble family. Its stone hall and outbuildings sit in a wooded valley; the surrounding estate grows grain and raises sheep. A small household of retainers and tenants keeps the manor in order.

name: Caras Galadhon
description: Caras Galadhon is the city of the Galadhrim in the heart of Lórien, built as a network of interconnected flets on the massive trunks of mallorn trees. Designed by Galadriel, it rises tier upon tier through the golden canopy; the highest flets look out over a sea of leaves lit from within by the light that never quite matches the light of the world outside. Mortals who enter feel simultaneously welcomed and observed; the city is safe but its beauty is unsettling.

name: Lond Thalos
description: Lond Thalos (S. Port of Thalos) served as both a medical and herbal community and as a timber port supplying wood to the shipyards of the Grey Havens.

name: Talsir
description: Talsir was one of the free cities of Cardolan on the west bank of the Gwathló, about 170 miles downstream from Tharbad. With a population of roughly 4,300 in T.A. 250, it was an important river stop and one of the few settlements of ancient Minhiriath to survive into the late Third Age.

name: Scary
description: Scary is a Hobbit mining and quarrying village in the rugged Eastfarthing, supplying most of the tin and copper ore used throughout the Shire. Its stone houses are sturdier than average for Hobbit settlements — the miners prefer solid construction — and the surrounding hills are pocked with mine shafts and quarry faces. The village has a slightly tougher character than most of the Shire: fewer flowers and more mud-boots.

name: Framsburg
description: Wraecaburg, though not directly under the Witch-King's rule, suffered frequent attacks and harbored many unsavory characters from outside Rhovanion. Its men occasionally traded with kinsmen at Maethelburg, but relations grew increasingly suspicious over time.

name: Endrist
description: Endrist was once a township of the highland region, but by the mid-Third Age its population had long since gone. The foundations and a few stretches of wall are all that remain — a quiet ruin avoided by those who know its history.

name: Annulond
description: The principal town of Pinnath Gelin, set at the tip of a tongue of land where a stream from the Ram Galen joins the Lefnui. The folk of Annúlond maintain the only bridge spanning the Lefnui in this region. The town is modest but orderly: small stone houses of one or two storeys line the streets, and the lieutenant-Cáno's large house stands beside the open main square that slopes down to the river wharf. Since the area lies in Gondor's peaceful interior, no town wall was ever built. Outside the walls, the Knight of Annúlond's manor overlooks liegemen's farms and tenant plots.

name: Carn Dûm
description: Carn Dûm sits at the northwestern tip of the Misty Mountains, the last great fortress of the Witch-king and the capital of Angmar. Its caverns and tunnels were originally delved by Dwarves; the Witch-king enlarged and fortified them enormously after T.A. 1300. Almost nothing is known of the interior — few of the Free Peoples who have approached it have returned. The mountain above it is perpetually dark with snow-cloud, and the road leading to the gate is guarded at every turn.

name: Eruimar
description: Eruimar (S. Lonely Home) sat at the foot of the Ramluin (S. Blue Wall), below the easternmost point where the Ered Luin foothills gave way to rocky cliffs. Despite its remote setting, distance rather than forest or enchantment created its isolation. The village held no more than thirty-five households; founded by the Dúnedain, most of its roughly 150 residents were River-men or Northmen by the mid-Third Age.

name: Haerlond
description: Haerlond is a small Gondorian harbour-town at the tip of Andrast, its modest dock serving ships damaged rounding the treacherous cape. Originally a Númenórean settlement of the Second Age, it received a royal charter from King Turambar in T.A. 602. The fishermen here are skilled at patching hulls and provisioning storm-battered vessels; their village is small and windswept, with whitewashed walls and a single street running down to the landing.

name: Annon Wain
description: Annon Wain, in the upper Lefnui valley, was a local market center. To the north lay extensive mines whose ore was shipped downriver to Annúlond, while nearby firs from the Ered Nimrais supplied Gondor's naval yards. The town itself was modest — merchant houses, simple dwellings, and riverside warehouses — with a population of roughly 2,500.

name: Thoronbar
description: Thoronbar was a village that did not survive the troubles of the middle Third Age. Its ruins are modest: a handful of stone foundations and a broken well, half-buried in turf on the edge of an old track.

name: Cerin Balgrist
description: Cerin Balgrist was once a city of consequence, but it has been a ruin for generations. The traveller finds only weathered stone, collapsed gateways and empty squares where a market presumably once met.

name: Andrath
description: A second ruined Andrath entry — the same site as id=1161, the administrative centre of Cardolan repeatedly sacked and rebuilt. In the mid-Third Age it is another ruin, its stones reused by whoever currently occupies the area.

name: Lorilad
description: Lórilad was a fortified village of Lebennin, situated in the Ethir Anduin roughly seventy-five miles downstream from Pelargir, halfway between Pelargir and the western cape of Lebennin.

name: Larad
description: Larad is a ruin on the edge of a river — the remains of a once-inhabited city now silent and empty. Its stone walls have partly fallen; the streets are overgrown and the only visitors are the occasional shepherd sheltering from rain.

name: Buzdum Mazar
description: The greatest mine of Nan-i-Naugrim and the capital of the Broadbeam Dwarves since the Second Age. By the mid-Third Age, Edennogrod — the largely ruined Khazad above Nan-i-Naugrim — dominated the area. At the end of the Third Age it was governed by the feudal lord Grár, who bore the honorary title Mountain King among the Shire-folk.

name: Minas Brethil
description: Minas Brethil (S. Silver Birch Tower), capital of Celossien, was situated in the upper Serni valley and served as an agricultural center between the Glinduin and Serni rivers. After the Kin-strife it held some 4,000 inhabitants, a number halved by the Great Plague and only slowly recovered thereafter.

name: Durthang
description: Durthang rests on a precarious ledge high above Udûn, giving its Gondorian garrison an excellent view of any movement across the plain below. Built by Isildur in T.A. 1, it is an uncomfortable posting — the winds off the Ephel Dúath are cold and bitter, the views are spectacular and grim, and the isolation is total. Supplies must be hauled up by ropes from the road below.

name: Melburg
description: Maethelburg (Meeting Fort) controlled virtually all trade through the upper Anduin valley and produced large quantities of tin from the lower Hithaeglir mines. Travelers heading west stocked up before crossing the Misty Mountains; those arriving from the east rested here. Built on an artificial mound behind a massive timber palisade, it harbored an unusually high number of spies due to its proximity to Angmar and the Orc-holds of Gundabad.

name: Iach Sarn
description: Iach Sarn clustered about the base of a limestone hill, surrounded by small farms and hardwood groves. On the south bank, just downstream of the main road, lay the ruins of an older village of earth-and-stone construction. Weathered steps led down a rubble embankment to the riverside docks, lined with unpainted ferries and fishing boats.

name: Cithrenor
description: Cithrenor is a ruined town, its streets grown over and its buildings collapsed to waist-height piles of dressed stone. A handful of travellers camp here when they must, but the place carries an atmosphere of abandonment that discourages lingering.

name: Maegoth
description: Maegoth was a city once, but the traveller finds only rubble. Crumbling walls, broken towers and overgrown foundations stretch for some distance across the hillside — a reminder of a time when this region was more populous and prosperous.

name: Enerond
description: Enerond is a ruined city on the east bank of the Lune, its stone buildings fallen and its population long since gone. It declined steadily through the Third Age; by T.A. 2050 there is not a soul living among the old walls.

name: Fanduin
description: Fanduin had a fairly large population of 4,100 in T.A. 250, but its numbers declined through the middle Third Age and the settlement was eventually abandoned before the end of the Third Age.

name: Pelargir
description: Pelargir (S. Garth of Royal Ships) was founded by the Faithful of Númenor in S.A. 2350 and became the chief harbour and oldest city of Gondor, built on the Anduin at the mouth of the Sirith. It served as the base of Gondor's great fleet and was the kingdom's primary port of trade, second city only to Minas Tirith.

name: Tir Lawen
description: Tir Lawen is a Noldorin Elvish city in the northern wilds, its tall spires visible from the surrounding hills as a point of light in an otherwise dark landscape. Founded in the early Third Age as an Elvish refuge, it is one of the few places of Noldorin habitation outside Lindon and Imladris. The city's stonework is of exceptional quality; its halls are bright and its people reserved toward mortal strangers.

name: Cor Angaladh
description: A ritual burial ground, now long disused. Once one of the most sacred sites of the Northmen, this place is now shadowed by the darkness of Mirkwood. Men of the north avoid it, believing it to be cursed.

name: Carrock
description: Carrock was a great rocky islet in the Anduin, between Iach Iaur and Maethelburg, held in deep spiritual reverence by the Beornings who dwelt nearby. The Anduin was shallow here, allowing a ford near the Carrock, though the swift current made the crossing genuinely dangerous.

name: Tuckborough
description: Tuckborough was the ancestral seat of the Took family, located in the Green Hill Country of Westfarthing. The surrounding area included the market center of Whitwell, the woodcraft village of Tuckburrow, and Dolwich, which supplied timber and granite to the southern Farthings.

name: Dol Caranthan
description: A town of Anórien on the west bank of the Anduin, north of Cair Andros, seat of the local noble family of House Caranthôn. Its stone houses face the great river, and the ferry landing here sees regular traffic of soldiers and merchants. The Caranthôn family's hall is the largest building in the settlement.

name: Barad Eldanar
description: An ancient Arnorian castle on the eastern edge of the Dyr Úvadan, ancestral home of House Eldanar — one of Elendil's Faithful. One of the oldest Númenórean fortifications in northwestern Middle-earth. By c. T.A. 1325, the Angmarim seized it; the rightful lord Elenuil escaped through a secret tunnel and the family has dwelt in Fornost since. A well-armed Angmarim garrison now holds the walls.

name: Buhr Mahrling
description: Buhr Mahrling is a Northman trading town on the lower River Celduin, seized from Easterlings in T.A. 1503. Most residents are seasonal Éothraim who use it as a base between their herding migrations; a sizeable community of Esgaroth Northmen maintain it as an advance trading outpost near Dorwinion. The result is a busy, polyglot town where Éothraim horse-traders, Northman merchants and the occasional Easterling trader haggle across a language barrier.

name: Endil
description: Endil is one of the chain of watch-towers maintained by the Prince of Dor-en-Ernil along the Belfalas coast, built by Anárion in the Second Age to warn of pirates and raiders. Its light is visible from ships approaching the rocky shoals; the signal is relayed along the chain to Dol Amroth within minutes. The garrison is small — a handful of men — but their position commands the most exposed stretch of the Belfalas coast.

name: Carach Angren
description: Carach Angren is the great iron gate that closes the pass between Udûn and Gorgoroth, its original Second Age construction by Sauron destroyed by the Last Alliance and rebuilt by Gondor in T.A. 42. The gate and its flanking walls completely block the pass; without permission to pass through, there is no way from Udûn into the interior of Mordor. The Gondorian garrison here is among the most unusual postings in the realm — surrounded on three sides by Mordor.

name: Bar-en-Dinnen
description: Bar-en-Dinnen (S. Dwelling on the Dinnen) was a fishing village and river-trading town in southern Ithilien, about sixty miles south of Minas Tirith, at the junction of the Tinnen and Anduin rivers. As the only significant settlement in Harithilien north of Hyarpende, it was an important hub for river commerce between Ithilien and Lebennin.

name: Mirolos
description: Mirolos (S. Jewel-Dream; also Londalos) was a Noldor settlement founded by followers of the Sons of Fëanor in the first decade of the Second Age, the largest community in the Hûb Conath region. Founded by Maglor before his disappearance, it was burned during the Long Winter when a northern dragon entered the area.

name: Forondir
description: Forondir has been a ruin for long enough that the locals no longer know its original name. Only broken walls and overgrown plazas remain of what must once have been a city of some importance.

name: Anhamar
description: The highland above the Anghir Vale where Anghul fortress stands — bleak moorland and dark rock, swept by the winds that give Anghul its name. No settlement of note, only military outposts and the occasional patrol of the Witch-king's men watching the No Man's Land.

name: Hobbiton
description: Hobbiton was a prominent Hobbit village in the Westfarthing of the Shire, north of the Great East Road. It was home to Bag End on the Hill — the residence of Bilbo Baggins and later Frodo Baggins.

name: Caras Tolfalas
description: Claimed by Gondor since S.A. 3321; first settled by royal command in T.A. 934.. Subsidized by Gondor's King more to maintain a claim on the Isle of Tolfalas than for economic reasons.. A poor harbor limits access to this town, but it is the only sure mooring on Tolfalas.

name: Buhr Waldmarh
description: Founded by Northmen, circa T.A. 1290.. A stable base for the Withras clan; an economic focus for the surrounding farms.. This typical Northman town has many timber longhouses and an earthen dike surmounted by a wooden palisade.

name: Buhr Widu
description: Founded by Vidugavia in T.A. 1249.. A center for administration for the Waildung clan; a collecting point for the surrounding Gramuz farms.. Buhr Widu, by the side of the small lake known as Woodmere, retains some of the royal splendor from the time of Vidugavia.

name: Barad Garan
description: Barad Garan stands as a ruin — towers toppled, walls breached — a shell of the fortress it once was. No garrison remains; wind moves through the open gateways and birds nest in the cracked stonework.

name: Cum Arelaid
description: Ruins. An ancient burial complex of pre-Númenórean tumuli and stone-lined graves, sacred to the early peoples of the region.

name: Barad-dûr
description: Raised by Sauron, circa S.A. 1000.. Domination of the plateau of Gorgoroth.. Built on a spur of the Ered Lithui, and fashioned by the power of Sauron's Ruling Ring, Barad-dûr consisted of a great central tower with nine radial towers connected to it by fortified bridges. No stronger fortress ever existed in Middle-earth. Once the seat of Sauron's power, Barad-dûr fell when Isildur cut the Ring from Sauron's hand. Orcs and Trolls now control the area, and no Man has visited Barad-dûr since the Plague.

name: Ethraid Engrin
description: The Fords of Isen were the main crossing point for those traversing the Gap of Rohan, with no bridge spanning the powerful river. The Rohirrim patrolled the area as their primary western gateway; Dunlending hillmen often seized the fords and demanded tolls. Three rows of broad flat stones crossed the waters, with graded horse-fords between them.

name: Annodrun
description: Annodrun was a city that did not survive the troubles of its age. Its ruins are extensive — collapsed walls, empty market squares and the stumps of old columns — but they are uninhabited and slowly disappearing under vegetation.

name: Rhosgobel
description: Built by Radagast around T.A. 1100.. A place of healing and herb-lore; a refuge from Mirkwood; an observation post on Dol Guldur.. Rhosgobel is a simple place formed out of the boughs of a great furry oak tree.

name: Argonath
description: Finished in T.A. 1340 by Romendacil II.. Designation of the northern boundary of Gondor.. Carved rocks flanking the chasm at the northern entrance to Nen Hithoel. The figures represent Isildur and Anárion. Since the time of Romendacil II, Gondor's northern border has moved even farther north, making the pillars obsolete. Nonetheless, they are a magnificent spectacle.

name: Amon Sûl
description: Originally built by Elendil in the Second Age.. Repository for a Palantír; later served as a landmark at the juncture of the three Kingdoms of the North.. From this vantage point, one has an excellent view of the Great East Road as it heads towards Rhudaur. For this reason, the hill served as a strategic focus for western Arthedain and Cardolan and has a long history of conflict surrounding it. The tower of Amon Sûl once held Palantír, which was removed to Fornost following the invasion by Angmar in T.A. 1409.

name: Ahus
description: A rough village of the Fell-men in Angmar, near the fortress of Angsûl. Its squat stone-and-timber huts sit in the shadow of the Witch-kingdom's northern hills, home to a tribe loyal to the Witch-king. The settlement smells of smoke and animal fat; life here is harsh and martial, and strangers are rarely welcome.

name: Dinach
description: A hilltop town in Mintyrnath, two days' walk northwest of Tharbad. It sits on a strategic spur overlooking the junction of the Sendiel and Andeithel rivers, where an old Arnorian stone bridge still spans the water. In the mid-Third Age, Dinach is a rough waystation: a small garrison holds the old tower, and the stone buildings along the Men Formen shelter travellers, smugglers and agents of various factions. The low-lying fields along the river flood every spring.

name: Dol Occum
description: A hill-tower on the Men Formen in southern Arthedain, within the village of Occum. The tower is a Dúnedain watch-post commanding the road; the surrounding village of timber and stone houses provides the garrison with supplies. The name derives from an older Westron name meaning 'ox-valley', and cattle are still the main wealth of the area.

name: Cargash
description: Built by Witch-king, ca. T.A. 1400.. Designed to give the Witch-king warning of trespassers and a base from which to strike at Arthedain.. Regular patrols depart from Mindil Cargash to sweep En Udanoriath which lies between Angmar and Arthedain.

name: Galibur
description: Settled during Númenórean expansion in Gondor, circa S.A. 2500.. An important Dunnish ritual site in central Belfalas peninsula.. Ruins dating to the time of the Daen Coentis lie just north of town.

name: Grimsly Dale
description: A Harfoot farming village located in a bend of the Fairwater River,quiet place, famous for its flowers . Although few remembered it , there had once been a scenic trail in the area that was a popular sidetrip on the Woodland Tour. Reason for its popularity could be discerned in the dense concentration of berry bushes and flowering plants along the riverside paths . The village got its name , however, from the bones of men and horses that also turned up along the trail . A large party of Dúnedain were ambushed here during the Scourging of T.A . 1409

name: Bankling
description: Known locally as 'Bankling' and later Whitfurrows, this good-sized Hobbit town sits squarely on the Great East Road in the midst of the best farmland of the Eastfarthing. Heavy wagons cross the Scary Pike at Budge Ford to the north, while a fine wooden bridge takes lighter traffic to Waterbury across the river. The town is known for its corn and flax fields and for the Blushing Ivy Tavern, meeting place of the Brow, Pen and Scroll Society — a bookish Hobbit club with a taste for Elvish lore that gives them a reputation for peculiarity among their neighbours.

name: Flynettle
description: Flynettle in the mid-Third Age is a rough river-landing and fishing camp on the Anduin — nothing more than a landing stage and a handful of sheds used by riverboat crews. The old Deep Hallow House is a crumbling ruin on the bank, avoided by locals.

name: Eldanar
description: Built by Númenórean colonists around S.A. 2800, seized by Angmar in T.A. 1325.. An advance watch-station for Angmar.. Built originally by the Dúnedain of Rhudaur, Eldanar is now a secure watch-station set well out into the plain known as the En Udanoriath.

name: Bottoms Bridge
description: Settled circa T.A. 1337.. A supply center for traders sailing on the Anduin.. Bottoms Bridge is located on a small island at the edge of the Gladden Marshes.

name: Derndingle
description: Used by Ents since the First Age.. A place of gathering and debate.. Derndingle is a dish-shaped valley with a small, tree-topped mound in the center.

name: Bauruin
description: Gobel Paurruin ('Village of the Firefist') was once a thriving township on the Uialduin, about seventy miles northwest of Annúminas. At its height in the early Third Age it held some 3,600 inhabitants. By T.A. 1400 it was abandoned. What a traveller finds today is a ghost of moss-covered foundations and broken walls reclaimed by scrub and willow — locals call it a haunted place and do not linger after dark.

name: Chalnen
description: A village that served as Saerlann's northernmost outpost along the Minsiril. In troubled times it is a rough place, known to mercenaries as 'Vice-town': a refuge for camp-followers, spies, deserters and opportunists. The local chieftain Cyllin does business with whoever pays. Stone huts and muddy lanes; a few alehouses that never close.

name: Cor Wilishar
description: A Nandorin settlement built on a high crag above the Baranduin and the Nenglos. The village was abandoned in the years following the Great Plague and the collapse of Cardolan (c. T.A. 1700). By the mid-Third Age, travellers approaching the crag find only the stone footings of old platforms and the rusted remains of rope-ladders; the Nandor who once lived here are gone.

name: Caras Denethora
description: An Elvish flet-village of central Harlindon, founded before the rising of the Sun by Danitharo — last king of the Green Elves. Hidden in the trackless ancient woods, it survived the Orc hunts of the First Age unscathed. In the Third Age it looks much as it did at the end of the First Age: wooden platforms and walkways built high among old trees, connected by rope-bridges, visible only when the lanterns are lit at dusk.

name: Bar-i-Dinuviel
description: A fishing village on the coast of Dor Tinuviel, its name meaning 'Home of the Nightingale'.
